sentence: Subjective:

HPI: The patient, a 56-year-old male, presents with a 4-5 week history of difficulty swallowing, describing a sensation of something being stuck in his throat, which is painful and occasionally requires him to wash down solid foods.
He reports no coughing, but occasionally experiences difficulty swallowing solid foods, particularly meat and steak, and sometimes needs to push them down.
He denies any blood in his stool, vomiting, or weight loss.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of diabetes and high blood pressure.
He is currently on metformin 500mg twice a day, and his last hemoglobin A1c was 7.4.
He has been taking his medications as prescribed and has made efforts to improve his diet and exercise.
He has not experienced any side effects from the metformin.
His blood pressure has been well-controlled, with a reading of 120/50 in the office today.
Review of systems: The patient denies any symptoms of nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or chest pain.
He reports no shortness of breath.
Current medications:
Metformin 500mg twice a day for diabetes
Norvasc (INDICATION NOT MENTIONED)
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 120/50, no other vital signs mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's neck is fine, with no swelling or masses.
The thyroid is normal, and there are no lymph nodes.
The throat examination is normal, with no masses or swelling.
The lung exam is clear, and the heart exam is normal.
The patient has epigastric tenderness on the right side, but no masses or significant swelling.
The back exam is normal, and the extremity exam is fine.
The neurological exam is normal.
Test Results:
Hemoglobin A1c 7.4 (last result, not current)
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient is presenting with difficulty swallowing, which may be related to a narrowing of the esophagus, potentially due to a stricture.
Problem list: Dysphagia; Diabetes Mellitus; Hypertension; Stricture of the Esophagus; Reflux Disease
Plan:
I prescribed Prilosec 20mg once a day for gastroesophageal reflux disease.
I recommended that the patient chew his foods frequently, especially solid foods like meat and steak, to prevent them from getting stuck.
I will refer the patient to gastroenterology for a scope to investigate the cause of the dysphagia.
I will order a repeat hemoglobin A1c, kidney function tests, and an eye referral to ophthalmology to check for diabetic retinopathy.
I will continue the patient on metformin and Norvasc, and will have the patient return in a month to review the results of the blood tests.
Follow-up: I will have the patient return in one month to review the results of the blood tests and make any necessary adjustments to his treatment plan.
